bibliographic_data.title: Do Privacy Policies Match with the Logs? An Empirical Study of Privacy Disclosure in Android Application Logs
bibliographic_data.abstract: Logs are essential resources of modern software systems, providing valuable information for software development activities, including debugging, performance monitoring, and security auditing. This paper presents an empirical study of patterns in log-related statements within privacy policies and their alignment with privacy disclosures observed in Android application logs. We analyzed 1,000 Android apps across multiple categories, generating 86,836,964 log entries. Our findings reveal that while most applications (88.0%) provide privacy policies, only 28.5% explicitly mention logging practices. Among those that reference logging, most clearly describe what information is logged; however, 27.7% of log-related statements remain overly simplistic or vague, offering limited insight into actual data collection. We further observed widespread privacy leakages in application logs, with 67.6% of apps leaking sensitive information not mentioned in their policies. Alarmingly, only 4% of applications demonstrated consistent alignment between declared policy contents and actual logged data.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study provides empirical insights into the discrepancies between privacy policies and logging practices in Android applications, highlighting the need for improved transparency mechanisms.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors analyzed 1,000 Android apps across multiple categories to identify patterns in log-related statements within privacy policies and their alignment with real-world logging behaviors. They generated 86,836,964 log entries to support their findings.
outcomes: Most applications (88.0%) provide privacy policies but only 28.5% explicitly mention logging practices.
Among those that reference logging, most clearly describe what information is logged; however, 27.7% of log-related statements remain overly simplistic or vague.
67.6% of apps leak sensitive information not mentioned in their policies.
Only 4% of applications demonstrate consistent alignment between declared policy contents and actual logged data.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper investigates the discrepancies between privacy policies and logging practices in Android applications through an empirical study. The authors analyzed 1,000 Android apps across multiple categories, generating 86,836,964 log entries to identify patterns in the disclosure of logging practices within privacy policies and their alignment with real-world logging behaviors. Key findings include: most applications (88.0%) provide privacy policies but only 28.5% explicitly mention logging practices; among those that reference logging, most clearly describe what information is logged; however, 27.7% of log-related statements remain overly simplistic or vague; and 67.6% of apps leak sensitive information not mentioned in their policies. Only 4% of applications demonstrate consistent alignment between declared policy contents and actual logged data. These findings highlight the need for improved transparency mechanisms and regulatory oversight to bridge the significant gap between privacy promises and actual data collection practices.
